coredata.dc:title: Metal-organic framework/carboxymethyl starch/graphene quantum dots ternary hybrid as a pH sensitive anticancer drug carrier for co-delivery of curcumin and doxorubicin
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of the Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Pooresmaeil, Malihe
Namazi, Hassan
coredata.dc:description:
Background The efficiency of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and glutamic acid-based metal-organic framework (Bio-MOF(Zn)) compounds have been reported in the drug delivery area. In addition, carboxymethyl starch (CMS) has a good performance in improving biocompatibility and obtaining a controlled anticancer drug release system. Fabrication of Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs can be hopeful in cancer treatment.
Methods In this work for the first time, a novel pH-sensitive and biocompatible drug carrier was prepared with the combination of CMS, GQDs, and Bio-MOF(Zn) for curcumin (CUR) and doxorubicin (DOX) co-delivery. For this, GQDs were prepared via pyrolysis of citric acid (CA). Then the CMS and GQDs mixture was crosslinked with zinc metal (Zn-CMS/GQDs). Finally, the Bio-MOF(Zn) was simply fabricated in the presence of L-glutamic acid (Glu) as proteinogenic amino acid and Zn-CMS/GQDs as a biocompatible platform (Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs).
Significant findings The formation of zinc-glutamate Bio-MOF in the presence of Zn-CMS/GQDs was elucidated by the common characterization techniques. Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) analysis obtained the pore diameter of Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs ternary hybrid at about 4.5 nm. The in vitro drug loading test obtained the loading percentage of CUR and DOX at about 54.2% and 43.2%. Moreover, the pH-sensitive drug release behavior was observed. Cellular tests confirmed the biocompatibility of Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs and its capability for cancer cell treatment after drug loading. A Survey of the published research works shows that the studied Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs as a DOX and CUR co-carrier have novelty from both components and application viewpoints. In conclusion, the obtained results can render the Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs ternary hybrid as a newly introduced smart candidate for anticancer drug delivery with great potential in biomedicine.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanocarrier
Carboxymethyl starch
Graphene quantum dots
Zinc-glutamate metal-organic frameworks
Co-drug delivery
Cancer treatment

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Chemical and Biomolecular Science and Technology

xocs:meta.xocs:copyright-line: © 2022 Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: In situ synthesis of Bio-MOF(Zn) in Zn-CMS/GQDs platform
CUR and DOX co-loading in Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs ternary hybrid and their releases
In vitro dual anticancer load and release study
A preliminary determination of in vitro cellular toxicity assay via MTT test

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2022 Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research is supported by a research grant of the University of Tabriz (number SAD/3930-14001225).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs fabrication and its potential for dual anticancer drug loading.
FT-IR spectra analysis of GQDs, CMS, Zn-CMS/GQDs, Bio-MOF(Zn), and Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs (a) Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs, CUR, DOX, and DOX@CUR@Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs.
X-ray diffraction patterns of GQDs, Zn-CMS/GQDs, pristine Bio-MOF(Zn), and Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs.

The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms (P/P 0, relative pressure) at 77 K of Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs (a) and pore size distribution plot taken from these isotherms (b), and fluorescence emission spectra of GQDs and Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs under an excitation wavelength of 360 nm (c).
SEM images of GQDs (a), Zn-CMS/GQDs (b), Bio-MOF(Zn) (c), and Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs (d).
EDX spectrum of Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs and table of EDX analysis (a) and EDX mapping images (b).

Zeta potential of GQDs, Zn-CMS/GQDs, Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs (a), also the in vitro cumulative release profiles of DOX (b) and CUR (c) from DOX@CUR@Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs at two different pHs; pH 7.4 and pH 5.0 and 37 °C. Data are represented as mean ± SD, n = 3.
The linear fitted curve of the DOX release from DOX@CUR@Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs with zero-order, first-order, Higuchi, and Korsmeyer-Peppas models in (pH 7.4, 37 °C) (a) and (pH 5.0, 37 °C) (b).
The linear fitted curve of the CUR release from DOX@CUR@Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs with zero-order, first-order, Higuchi, and Korsmeyer-Peppas models in (pH 7.4, 37 °C) (a) and (pH 5.0, 37 °C) (b).

HepG2 cell viability for Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs as a tool for measuring its biocompatibility (a), for DOX@CUR-loaded Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs, and pure DOX@CUR solution for measuring anticancer efficacy of DOX@CUR (b).

Fluorescence microscopy images of nuclear morphology in HepG2 cells after 48 h exposure to the untreated cells (control, a), Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs (b), free DOX@CUR (c), and DOX@CUR@Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs. The scale bar is 20 μm.
R 2 and n values for DOX and CUR release from DOX@CUR@Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs according to zero order, first order, Higuchi, and Korsmeyer–Peppas models.

head.ce:title: Metal-organic framework/carboxymethyl starch/graphene quantum dots ternary hybrid as a pH sensitive anticancer drug carrier for co-delivery of curcumin and doxorubicin

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Laboratory of Dendrimers and Nano-Biopolymers, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, P.O. Box 51666, Tabriz, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Laboratory of Dendrimers and Nano-Biopolymers, Faculty of Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center for Pharmaceutical Nanotechnology, Biomedicine Institute, Tabriz University of Medical Science, Tabriz, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Research Laboratory of Dendrimers and Nano-Biopolymers, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, P.O. Box 51666, Tabriz, Iran.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Laboratory of Dendrimers and Nano-Biopolymers, Faculty of Chemistry

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The efficiency of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and glutamic acid-based metal-organic framework (Bio-MOF(Zn)) compounds have been reported in the drug delivery area. In addition, carboxymethyl starch (CMS) has a good performance in improving biocompatibility and obtaining a controlled anticancer drug release system. Fabrication of Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs can be hopeful in cancer treatment.

In this work for the first time, a novel pH-sensitive and biocompatible drug carrier was prepared with the combination of CMS, GQDs, and Bio-MOF(Zn) for curcumin (CUR) and doxorubicin (DOX) co-delivery. For this, GQDs were prepared via pyrolysis of citric acid (CA). Then the CMS and GQDs mixture was crosslinked with zinc metal (Zn-CMS/GQDs). Finally, the Bio-MOF(Zn) was simply fabricated in the presence of L-glutamic acid (Glu) as proteinogenic amino acid and Zn-CMS/GQDs as a biocompatible platform (Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs).

The formation of zinc-glutamate Bio-MOF in the presence of Zn-CMS/GQDs was elucidated by the common characterization techniques. Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) analysis obtained the pore diameter of Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs ternary hybrid at about 4.5 nm. The in vitro drug loading test obtained the loading percentage of CUR and DOX at about 54.2% and 43.2%. Moreover, the pH-sensitive drug release behavior was observed. Cellular tests confirmed the biocompatibility of Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs and its capability for cancer cell treatment after drug loading. A Survey of the published research works shows that the studied Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs as a DOX and CUR co-carrier have novelty from both components and application viewpoints. In conclusion, the obtained results can render the Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs ternary hybrid as a newly introduced smart candidate for anticancer drug delivery with great potential in biomedicine.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer is one of the life-threatening diseases which extremely affect the well-being and life quality of numerous people [1–4]. Chemotherapy is one of the main and commonly used approaches in cancer treatment [5–7]. Up now different types of chemotherapy drugs have been used for cancer treatment. DOX is one main member of the chemotherapeutic drugs which has been used for treating various types of cancers for instance stomach, breast, lungs, lymphomas, leukemia, etc. This drug can kill cancer cells by inhibiting topoisomerase IIβ via chelation of DNA [8–12]. However, because of the heterogeneity and complex structure of the tumors which have significant drug resistance in most cases the use of a single anticancer drug is inadequate for cancer treatment. CUR is another chemotherapy drug that induces cellular apoptosis and could be used in a combined form with DOX [13–16]. The Co-delivery of dual or multi drugs has significant toxicity to various cancer cells owing to their special activity at different stages of tumor growth than the individual one [17]. Despite the ability of chemotherapy to kill cancer cells, the direct administration of anticancer drugs also brings some unwanted side effects to healthy cells. Therefore, emerging new co-drug delivery systems that can deliver two or more drugs and eliminate the drug-resistant turmeric cells for obtaining higher efficiency by using lower doses of therapeutic agents is essential and has great importance in chemotherapy [2]. In between, nanoparticles (NPs) are multifunctional materials and have become an interesting co-delivery system for chemotherapeutic drugs [18–21]. Carbon-based nanomaterials, especially GQDs, are in the prime position of the nanomaterial list as compared to others due to their special characteristics [22–25]. Small size (<10 nm), photostability, biocompatibility, eco-friendly, excellent intrinsic fluorescence ability, excellent photostability, abundant surface functional groups, great surface area, biocompatibility, and fabrication from cost-effectiveness raw materials are some advantages of GQDs [26–29]. Outstandingly, GQDs containing systems can provide higher delocalized π electrons which are favorable for π-π interactions with drug molecules, and also they have a large potential for interaction through their large number of oxygen-containing functional groups [26,30,31]. Nowadays, bio-nanocomposites are known to offer many improved features owing to the incorporation of nanomaterials [32]. Polysaccharides like starch have gained significant attention in the prepared nanocomposites for use in drug delivery [33–35]. But they suffer from some limitations like insolubility in cold water and a tendency to aggregate in an aqueous medium [36,37]. Among several derivatives of starch, the CMS is a low-cost gel-forming anionic polymer, obtained by carboxymethyl (-CH2-COOR) modification on naturally starch which offers structural integrity and is best defined as the raw material for preparing drug carriers [38,39]. The association of MOFs with biocompatible polymers could be a versatile strategy to make the new hybrid porous materials in a synergistic combination of their respective features. MOFs are a new class of porous crystalline materials with satisfying attributes like rich composition, well-defined topological structures, regular porosity, and large specific surface area [40]. Among these, the MOFs made from green solvents and natural feedstock could bring environmental compatibility as well as, they can favor a sustainable and easy scale-up [41]. The systems prepared from synthetic polymers, chemical crosslinkers, mineral quantum dots (QDs), and MOF based on non-natural ligands suffer from toxicity problems and high cost in most cases which establishes the need for a new study in drug carrier designing. Therefore, in our ongoing program, related to the development of a new drug delivery system this work tries to use the advantage of using biopolymers, physical crosslinking, natural GQDs, and amino acid-based Bio-MOF at once. On the other hand, the facile synthesis at room temperature, use of low-cost reagents, and the green solvent is the comparative advantage of this research with the previously published works [42–44].

In deed, thanks to the complementary features between photoluminescent GQDs, biocompatible nature of CMS, and porous structure of Bio-MOFs, and inspired by the published research, in this work, the novel Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs was first synthesized for co-delivery of anticancer drugs. To the best of the authors’ knowledge, at the present time, there are no reports available in the literature on the synthesis of Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs for the co-delivery of CUR and DOX. Hence, the studied Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs as a DOX and CUR carrier in the current research has novelty from both components and application viewpoints. In this regard, initially, combining the advantages of GQDs and CMS, new photoluminescent zinc crosslinked system was prepared by a physical crosslinking technique. Then the Bio-MOF(Zn) was in-situ synthesized in the presence of Zn-CMS/GQDs by an eco-friendly and economic route at room temperature in which zinc centers are connected by Glu units. The produced system showed a high specific surface area and a rich pore structure. The system exhibited the pH-controlled drug release which not only prevents the side effects on normal cells but also increases the treatment efficiency. Finally, the biocompatibility of the Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs was approved.

Potassium bromide (KBr), CA, sodium hydroxide (NaOH), starch, isopropanol, monochloroacetic acid, methanol, silver nitrate, acetic acid, hydrochloric acid (HCl), acetone, zinc sulfate heptahydrate (ZnSO4.7H2O), and Glu were supplied from Merck. Bromophenol blue, 4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI), CUR, 3-(4, 5-dimethylthiazole-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT), and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Fetal bovine serum albumin (FBS), trypsin, penicillin-streptomycin, and Roswell Park Memorial Institute (RPMI) were provided from Gibco, USA. DOX was obtained from Sobhan Pharmaceuticals Co. (Iran). Double distilled water was utilized in the overall process. All used materials were of analytical grade and used without any previous treatments unless otherwise stated.

The surface morphology and elemental distribution of the fabricated samples were analyzed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and energy dispersive X-Ray (EDX) analysis employing a scanning electron microscope (MIRA3 FEG-SEM, Tescan). SEM and EDX experiments were conducted at an accelerating voltage of 15 kV. In order to observe the real structure of the samples, a thin layer of gold was sputter-coated before scanning. The structures of samples were investigated using Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy measurement employing Bruker Instruments (FT-IR, model Aquinox 55, Germany). For the FT-IR analysis, the samples were mixed with KBr powder, pressed into tablets, and then the spectra were obtained at 400–4000 cm−1 with a resolution of 4 cm−1. The surface area and porosity of the product were recorded via Sorptometer (BELsorp, Japan) after the determination of the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms at 77 K. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of the dried samples was determined on an XRD instrument (Siemens diffractometer, D5000-Germany) with Cu Ka-α radiation (λ = 0.154 nm) at 2θ = 5–70° and a scanning rate of 1 °/min. An ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometer (Shimadzu 1700, Japan) was used to obtain the ultraviolet-visible (UV-Vis) spectra. The zeta potential analysis was performed employing a DLS-ZP/Particle Sizer (Microtrac, model Nanotrac Wave, USA). The pH and photoluminescence (PL) investigations respectively were performed employing a PHS-3CpH meter)China(and Jasco FP-8300 spectrofluorimeter (Japan) at room temperature. A fluorescence microscope (Bh2-RFCA, Olympus, Japan) was used to detect the nucleus morphology.

GQDs were synthesized through the pyrolysis of CA according to the previously reported procedure [45]. Generally, CA (2 g) was put into a ceramic vessel and then warmed at 200 °C for 30 min employing a heating mantle. The change in the state and color of the CA powder from white powder to orange melted product displayed the formation of GQDs. The pH of the obtained orange liquid was neutralized at 7.0 through the drop-by-drop addition of it into an aqueous solution of NaOH. The solvent of the GQDs solution was evaporated under reduced pressure by employing a rotary evaporator. Eventually, drying in a vacuum oven obtained the GQDs powder which was kept in the dark vial for further usage.

CMS was prepared via etherification of the starch according to the Refs. [46,47] with some modifications. Initially, about 2.4 g of NaOH was added to 44 mL of H2O/isopropanol (15:85,). Afterward, starch gradually was poured into the reaction medium. The temperature of the reaction was set at 60 °C and the reaction was followed via the addition of monochloroacetic acid (3 g, for 30 min) while vigorously stirred. After 2 h stirring at 60 °C, the solvent was decanted and the produced CMS was suspended in aqueous methanol overnight. The attained precipitate was neutralized with acetic acid (10% v/v). The achieved CMS was purified through several washing steps with methanol to remove the unreacted raw materials. Washing with methanol was continued to observe a negative answer to the silver nitrate solution. Lastly, the product was dried at 60 °C and then a fine white powder of CMS was obtained after crushing in a mortar. Titration of the obtained CMS with HCl (0.1 M) and bromophenol blue in a mixture of acetone/H2O determined the degree of substitution (DS) of the synthesized CMS to about 0.44 (DS = 0.44).

For the Zn-CMS/GQDs synthesis, about 50 mL of CMS solution was prepared with the dissolving of 1.5 g CMS in distilled water containing GQDs (30%). In the following, ZnSO4.7H2O aqueous solution (20 mL, 1 M) was added dropwise to the CMS/GQDs mixed solution with continuous stirring at room temperature. The reaction was continued for 2 h and a suspension was obtained. The suspension was centrifuged and the obtained precipitate was washed with distilled water. Thereafter, the obtained precipitate was freeze-dried, powdered, and kept at 4 for further use [48].

ce:section.ce:section-title: In situ synthesis of Bio-MOF(Zn) in Zn-CMS/GQDs platform
ce:section.ce:para: Glu (0.294 g, 2 mmol) was dissolved in 10 mL of NaOH solution and then about 0.2 g of Zn-CMS/GQDs fine powder was dispersed in it. The mixture magnetically was stirred at room temperature for 24 h. The product was eliminated by centrifuge, washed with water, and then vacuum dried to obtain Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs [49].

ce:section.ce:section-title: CUR and DOX co-loading in Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs ternary hybrid and their releases
ce:section.ce:para:
The DOX@CUR loaded Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs was prepared using two sequential-step processes. First CUR (500 ppm) was dissolved in ethanol (10 mL). Subsequently, the CUR solution was added to the Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs (30 mg) and the mixture was shaken overnight in darkness. After 24 h, the mixture was centrifuged and the CUR-loaded sample was washed with ethanol to eliminate the unloaded CUR. In the following, the CUR@Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs was immersed in DOX solution (5 mg DOX; 10 mL PBS). After 24 h shaking in the dark condition, the obtained DOX@CUR@Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs was centrifuged, and unloaded DOX molecules were washed with PBS buffer to remove. The final product was obtained through vacuum drying. Next to each step, the supernatant was collected to determine the drug loading content employing UV-Vis spectroscopy at their wavelength of maximum absorbance (λmax) and by using a standard curve. Finally, Eqs. (1) and (2) were used to calculate the drug loading capacity and drug loading efficiency.
(1)
Drug
loading
capacity
(
% w
)
=
Mass
of
drug
in
carrier
Mass
of
nanocarrier
×
100
(2)
Drug
entrapment
efficiency
(
% w
)
=
Mass
ofdrug
in
carrier
Mass
of
drug
fed
initially
x
100

The in vitro co-drug release behavior of the DOX@CUR@Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs was studied under two different pHs, pH 7.4 and pH 5.0. As the typical procedure of drug release, the DOX@CUR@Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs were dispersed in a buffer solution with specific pH (10 mL) and then shaken in dark conditions. At certain time intervals, 3 mL of the release medium was taken to estimate the drug's concentration by employing a UV-Vis spectrophotometer. Besides, after each sampling, immediately 3 mL of corresponding fresh buffer was poured into the drug release vessel to guarantee that the total volume of the release medium stayed constant. The release percentage of both drugs from the DOX@CUR@Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs was calculated using the following formula (Eq. (3)).
(3)
Drug
release
(
% w
)
=
Amount
of
released
drug
Amount
of
loaded
drug
×
100

To reveal the mechanism which affects the drug releases, the resulting drug release data were fitted with four kinetic models; zero-order (Eq. (4)), first-order (Eq. (5)), Higuchi (Eq. (6)), and Korsmeyer–Peppas (Eq. (7)) models [50]. The determined correlation coefficient (R 2) after fitting with each model was compared and the model with the highest R 2 value was selected as the best-fitting model with the experimental data. Furthermore, the obtained value of the “n” parameter revealed the drug release mechanism. So, n= 1, 0.45<n > 1, and n ≤ 0.45 were observed respectively when the drug release follow case-II transport, non-Fickian transport, and Fickian diffusion.
(4)
Q =
k 0
t
(5)
lnQ = l n
Q 0
−
k
1
t
(6)
Q =
k H
t
1 2
(7)
lnQ = n l n t + Ln
k p

The k 0, k 1, kH , and kP symbols in the above equations Eqs. (4)–(7) respectively denote the rate constants of the zero-order, first-order, Higuchi, and Korsmeyer–Peppas kinetic models. As well as the amount of the released drug depicted with Q.

HepG2 cells were selected as an in vitro experimental model of the cancer cell line to accomplish the cytotoxicity test by doing the MTT procedure. Typically, the cells at an initial concentration of 1 × 104 cells per well were cultured in RPMI medium supplemented with 10% FBS and 1% penicillin-streptomycin solution and then incubated in a humidified 5% CO2 incubator at 37 °C overnight. After attaching about 80% of the cells to the bottom of the wells, the culture medium was removed and the samples with different concentrations were added to each plate and incubated for 48 h. The unused sample was removed and 200 µL of MTT solution (5 mg/mL) was added to each well. After incubation for 4 h, the supernatant of wells was taken out and DMSO (200 µL) was added to dissolve the formed purple formazan crystals. Lastly, the optical density (OD) value of each well was obtained by ELISA plate reader at 570 nm with a reference wavelength of 630 nm to calculate the cell viability rate. In the MTT assay, the untreated cells were named as a control group.

DAPI nuclear staining test was accomplished to monitor the chromatin morphological and density changes by visualizing the HepG2 cell nuclei with fluorescence microscopy after 48 h treatment. In this regard, the cells were seeded in 6 well plates and incubated overnight. Afterward, the seeded cells were treated with each sample and then further incubated for 48 h. The treated cells were rinsed with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) and fixed with paraformaldehyde (3 wt%). Then the cells were permeabilized by adding Triton X-100 and incubated for 10 min. The nuclei of the cells were stained with DAPI solution. Lastly, the stained cells were observed using a fluorescence microscope (Bh2-RFCA, Olympus, Japan) to detect DNA fragmentation and condensation in apoptotic cells.

The incorporation of three or more portions in one drug delivery system is very interesting because this way can provide a synergistic capability for tumor treatment. Therefore in this work, the Zn-GQDs/CMS was prepared via crosslinking of GQDs and CMS mixture with zinc metal as a physical crosslinker. Then the prepared Zn-GQDs/CMS was immersed in Glu solution as a biocompatible linker to construct the bio-based MOF in GQDs/CMS platform. The overall procedures for the synthesis of Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs are described in Fig. 1 . To reveal the formation of Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs, some well-known characterization techniques were performed in detail. Thereafter the CUR and DOX were loaded on MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs and its potential as an anticancer co-drug carrier and in vitro anticancer activity were evaluated.

FT-IR analysis was carried out to study the performed step-by-step reactions for the Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs fabrication (Fig. 2 a) via analysis of the chemical structures. In the FT-IR spectrum of GQDs, the absorption bands were observed at 1122 cm−1 (due to -OH stretching vibration), at 1393 cm−1 (due to symmetric stretching vibrations of -COO−), at 3031 cm−1 (due to stretching vibration of the -CH), and at 3248 cm−1 (due to -OH stretching vibration). The observation of the peak around 1600–1800 cm−1 (due to the merging of the stretching vibration of C=C and asymmetric stretching frequency of -COO−) indicates the conversion of CA into GQDs. The FT-IR spectrum of CMS presented the peaks at 1602 cm−1 and 1421 cm−1 which approve that some of the hydroxyl groups of the starch biopolymer are in carboxymethylated form. So, the CMS showed a broad absorptions band around 3419 cm−1 (due to the presence of hydroxyl functional groups), the band at 2927 cm−1 (due to stretching vibrations of the C-H), at 1602 cm−1 (due to asymmetric stretching vibration of -COO−), at 1421 cm−1 (due to symmetric stretching vibration of –COO−), and the broad bands at 1095–1016 cm−1 (due to polysaccharide skeleton absorption). After cross-linking the CMS and GQDs mixture with Zn2+ as a physical crosslinker, the absorption bands at 1602 cm−1 shifted to a higher wavenumber and can be observed at 1630 cm−1. This observation could be related to the formation of the ionic bond between the cationic Zn2+ ions and deprotonated carboxylate groups of CMS, resulting in a Zn-CMS/GQDs crosslink formation. Moreover, the appearing peaks at 400 to 550 cm−1 could be related to Zn-O vibration as a result of the physically cross-linking with Zn2+. For Bio-MOF(Zn), the asymmetric and symmetric vibration of carboxyl groups appeared at 1557 cm−1 and 1400 cm−1, which indicated that the carboxyl groups coordinated with the Zn ion. Moreover, the single peak at 3350 cm−1 and 3256 cm−1 related to the N-H stretching vibration of the secondary amine, thereby suggesting that the N-H in Glu is in a coordinated state with the metal Zn ion [49,51]. After the growth of Bio-MOF(Zn) in the Zn-GQDs/CMS platform, one absorption band at 1508 cm−1 was observed. This peak was attributed to the vibration of the N-H, suggesting the existence of Bio-MOF(Zn) in the composites of Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs.

FT-IR analysis was also performed to confirm the successful loading of CUR and DOX on Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs via analysis of the DOX@CUR@Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs chemical compositions. CUR was characterized by the absorption bands at 1276 cm−1 (due to stretching vibrations of alcoholic C-O), at 1431 cm−1 (due to bending vibration of alkane C-H), at 1508 cm−1 (due to C=C of the aromatic ring), at 1626 cm−1 (due to carbonyl of enol group), at 2924 cm−1 (due to stretching vibration of -CH), at 3506 cm−1 (due to stretching vibration of hydroxyl groups of phenolic compounds), and also at 857 cm−1 and 812 cm−1 (due to out-of-plane bending vibration of meta- and para-substituted aromatic systems). In the FT-IR spectrum of DOX, the main absorption peaks were detected at 3422 cm−1 (due to stretching vibration of -OH), at 2920 cm−1 (due to stretching vibration of C-H), at 1724 cm−1 (due to stretching vibration of C=O), at 1117 cm−1 (due to C-O-C vibration), and at 889 cm−1 (due to vibrations of N-H). As seen in Fig. 2b, the DOX@CUR@Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs spectrum, display the characteristic peaks of CUR, DOX, and Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs spectra and it can be concluded that the CUR and DOX were successfully co-loaded into the Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs. All of the above FT-IR results prove a successful stepwise nanocomposite fabrication as expected and dual drug molecules loading on it.

The crystalline structures of the prepared samples were investigated by XRD, as presented in Fig. 3 . This analysis was utilized to examine whether the Bio-MOF(Zn) was fabricated in the Zn-CMS/GQDs platform. Evidently, GQDs showed a broad peak at 2θ 24° (002) of its XRD pattern which could be assigned to its hexagonal lattice structure. Using the Bragg equation the interlayer spacing was calculated at about 3.7 Å which is similar to the previously obtained value for the graphitic materials and reveals its formation via CA pyrolysis. The XRD diffraction patterns of the Zn-CMS/GQDs displayed a broad peak in the 2θ range of 18°–28°, a characteristic of non-crystalline material. As-produced Bio-MOF(Zn) XRD pattern was matched properly to the previously reported XRD pattern [49]. Then, the XRD spectrum of Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs was recorded. This spectrum showed some of the main characteristic peaks of Bio-MOF(Zn) which is attributable to the interpenetration of the Bio-MOF(Zn) in the Zn-CMS/GQDs platform through the possible electrostatic interactions and can be a good sign for its successful synthesis within the Zn-CMS/GQDs platform. The lower intensity of the Bio-MOF(Zn) XRD peak compared to the substrate can be attributed to its lower percentage compared to the polymer

Considering the pore size effects on the drug loading capability, the N2 adsorption/desorption pattern of the Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs was obtained to evaluate its porosity. The achieved isotherm is displayed in Fig. 4 . The isotherm of Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs exhibited the presence of narrow pores in its structure. The BET surface area and pore volume for Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs were 8.39 m2/g and 1.93 mL/g, respectively. The examination of the pore size distribution plot obtained the average pore diameter ∼4.5 nm. Compared with CUR and DOX size, the pore size (4.5 nm) of Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs was higher, which suggested that the drug molecules can be loaded through occupation in the Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs cavity. Overall the BET results can confirm the ability of the prepared system for loading the anti-cancer drug molecules on it, which is the main needing criteria for each drug delivery system.

The fluorescence emission spectra of the GQDs and Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs in the maximum excitation wavelengths of 360 nm were obtained at 25 °C. The achieved spectra are shown in Fig. 4c. According to Fig. 4c, the maximum emission wavelengths of GQDs and Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs, respectively, are obtained at about 460 nm and 405 nm. The observation of wider emission peak with slightly shifting and low intensity for Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs in comparison with GQDs indicates the possible self-absorbing, filter effect, intersystem transitions, external or internal transformations phenomena and could be the reasons for the success in Bio-MOF(Zn) synthesis in Zn-CMS/GQDs platform. For further confirmation, the digital photo of the Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs dispersion in water was obtained under 360 nm UV light. As presented in the inset of Fig. 4c, the appearance of the blue emission approve the photoluminescent property of Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs and its suitability for bioimaging application. The appearance of PL characteristics is the main toll to approving the introduction of GQDs in the final system.

The morphology assessment of each drug delivery vehicle is one of the most important because it can affect the drug load and release behavior. The taken SEM images of the GQDs, Zn-CMS/GQDs, Bio-MOF, and Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs are exposed in Fig. 5 . Uniform spherical particles are visible in the SEM image of GQDs with an average diameter of about 5–6 nm [5,52]. However, SEM images of the Zn-CMS/GQDs show a relatively different morphology, which is probably because of the Zn2+ crosslinking. So, the cross-linking led to relatively closer interaction between the biopolymer chains and make them closer. Also, GQDs can be seen to be distributed in the CMS matrix. The crystalline nature was observed for Bio-MOF(Zn) in its SEM imaging, approving the obtained XRD technique results. The mean diameter of Bio-MOF(Zn) is in the range of 100–130 nm. In comparison with pure Zn-CMS/GQDs, several other new NPs are present on the surfaces of Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs which can be a reason for Bio-MOF(Zn) synthesis in the Zn-CMS/GQDs platform. Moreover, SEM images show that the surface of the Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs is highly wrinkled with many cavities which can help to easier diffusion of buffered solution into carrier structure and CUR@DOX release occur.

In the following, the surface chemical composition of the Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs was explored by EDX to further evaluate the chemical purity of the sample and Bio-MOF(Zn) dispersion on it. The elemental composition and chemical states of the prepared sample are shown in Fig. 6 . The detection of all of the expected elements; carbon (C), oxygen (O), zinc (Zn), and nitrogen (N) elements peaks in the EDX spectrum of Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs approved the presence of Bio-MOF(Zn) within it. As well as the obtained map disclosed the homogeneous scattering of Zn and N elements due to the presence of Bio-MOF(Zn) within the Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs.

The success of the performed reactions was monitored by observing the step-by-step changes of the zeta potential for the synthesized samples. GQDs showed a negative charge in the zeta potential from -18.2 mV, whereby the presence of carboxylic and hydroxyl groups in its structure. As illustrated in Fig. 7 a in the case of zinc crosslinked CMS/GQDs the zeta potential was shifted to + 1 mV due to the existence of Zn2+ in its structure. The changes in the zeta potentials (+16.5 mV) after Bio-MOF(Zn) growth on Zn-CMS/GQDs exhibited that the Bio-MOF(Zn) was successfully fabricated in Zn-CMS/GQDs platform and can control the surface charge of the final product. This observation is due to the presence of the amine functional groups on the Bio-MOF(Zn) structure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro dual anticancer load and release study
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the efficacy of the synthesized Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs as a newly fabricated anticancer dual drug carrier, the co-delivery of CUR and DOX as two models of anticancer drugs was studied on it. The drug encapsulation efficiency and drug loading capacity of Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs were found by UV-Vis spectrometer to be 54.2% and 9.03% for CUR and 43.2% and 7.2% for DOX, respectively. Typically the observed value can be attributed to some possible interactions. (I) the π–π interaction between aromatic rings of GQDs and also Bio-MOF(Zn) with the aromatic ring of the drug molecules. (II) formation of the electrostatic and strong hydrogen bonding between polar functional groups of both drugs with NH2, OH, and COOH groups on Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs. (III) Trapping of the drug molecules in the pore size of the final system.

In the following, to verify the capability of the prepared system in controlled cancer therapy, the release behavior of the dual drug-loaded system was studied in both cancer and physiological cell conditions. The results were presented in Fig. 7b and c. The in vitro release profile of CUR and DOX from DOX@CUR@Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs displayed a biphasic release with rapid release in the first 12 h followed by sustained release until 72 h (Fig. 8 a and b). The sustained release profile might be because of the encapsulated drug molecule's diffusion from the porous structure of the carrier. It is evident from the observed results that the rate of drug release was decreased with pH increase; tends to deliver the drugs in a controlled manner. A significantly lower release of both drugs at pH 7.4 in comparison with that of pH 5.0 was observed and the cumulative release within 72 h just was 45.2% (CUR) and 51.8% (DOX) and 30.8% (CUR) and 37.0% (DOX), respectively, at pH 5.0 and pH 7.4 after 72 h incubation. This phenomenon resulted from the loading of the drugs in a stimuli-responsive nanocarrier, which restricted the early leakage of drug molecules and minimized drug loss before reaching the target site. The outcome shows that the use of Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs as an anticancer drug delivery system can reduce non-specific distribution and side effects. Of course, DOX showed a quicker release rate than CUR which might be related to their different structure. Weakening the π–π stacking interaction between the aromatic ring of drug carrier and both drugs and an increase in the solubility of drugs at acidic pH are reasonable explanations for the observed fast drug release was more sensitive to pH variation. Additionally, at pH 7.4, hydrogen-bonding interaction between both drug and carrier is strong which results in the low release of both drugs at this pH. It could be concluded that Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs possessed a stable structure under normal physiological conditions, but the structure facilitated the pH-responsive drug release. These findings show that the Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs could be a favorable controlled pH-responsive co-drug delivery system for cancer therapy.

Kinetic of the DOX and CUR release from the DOX@CUR@Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs was elucidated via linearly fitting of the obtained in vitro experimental data with four common mathematical kinetic models to describe the mechanism of drug releases. The obtained results regarding the pharmacokinetic study are provided in Figs. 8 and 9 . Moreover, the n and correlation coefficient (R 2) were also tabulated, as presented in Table 1 . From determined R 2, we get to know the model which is the best fit for the obtained release data. As it is presented DOX@CUR@Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs showed good regression values, when plotted in accordance with the Higuchi kinetics equation. In the subsequent, the determined “n” value was assessed to further identify the mechanism of both drug diffusion. Considering the obtained release exponent, values; “n” parameter for all tested conditions was obtained about 0.45–1.0 the drug release follows the non-Fickian mechanism at all studied conditions. In summary, the prepared drug delivery system based on CMS, GQDs, and Bio-MOF(Zn) combination showed that the release of both CUR and DOX followed the non-Fickian diffusion mechanism with n 0.45–1.0.

ce:section.ce:section-title: A preliminary determination of in vitro cellular toxicity assay via MTT test
ce:section.ce:para: For drug delivery applications, the cytotoxicity assay is crucial while introducing each new synthetic formulation to avoid possible undesirable cytotoxic effects. In this regard, cellular cytotoxicity of the prepared Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs, DOX@CUR, and DOX@CUR@Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs was examined toward the HepG2 cell line after incubation with a specific concentration of the samples for 48 h by a direct contact MTT assay. Compared to the untreated cells, the results revealed a decrease in the MTT signal for the treated cells, as well as the dose-dependent pattern, was detected for all samples. According to Fig. 10 , in comparison with DOX@CUR the DOX@CUR@Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs can be more effective against studied cells and inhibit cell proliferation. So, the IC50 analysis showed that the 2.68 μg/mL concentration of DOX@CUR@Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs was the dose at which ∼50% of HepG2 cell growth was inhibited, compared to the control. Fig. 10b shows the IC50 values of the DOX@CUR@Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs formulations and free DOX@CUR. Moreover, the non-cytotoxic effect was detected for the Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs treated HepG2 cells after 48 h, indicating its relative biocompatibility and safety. So the cell viability rates were still about 87.5% after incubation with a high concentration of it. Overall the achieved results suggest the biocompatibility of Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs and improving the therapeutic effect of DOX@CUR after loading on it which can also reduce the needing dose and could be proposed as an effective formulation for the hepatocellular carcinoma cells treatment.

DAPI staining test was performed to visualize the chromatin condensation and crescent-shaped nuclei within the nuclear membrane of the treated cells displaying the typical chromatin condensation of apoptosis or safety in the nucleus (Fig. 11 ). The morphological changes in the treated cells with different formulations compared with the morphology of the untreated cells (control group). The obtained images showed that the chromatin condensation, cell shrinkage, and nuclear fragmentation intensity were enhanced when the cells were treated with DOX@CUR-loaded Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs than DOX@CUR in the same dosages. But importantly, the morphology of cancer cells was not influenced by the addition of Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs, thereby indicating satisfactory biocompatibility of it.

In conclusion, in the present work, a biocompatible and economically smart drug carrier on a natural source was successfully developed and studied for controlled co-delivery of two model drugs; DOX and CUR in HepG2 cancer cells. For this purpose, first, the CMS was physically crosslinked with zinc metal in the presence of pre-synthesized GQDs. Then the dispersion of in Glu solution created a new material with the photoluminescent property, biocompatibility, and puros structure. Because there is no study on the synthesis and evaluation of Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs potentials as drug delivery agents; it seems that the evaluation of this and upcoming systems have acceptable novelty in the field of controlled drug release. The produced nanosystem was characterized in terms of having the desired structure via FT-IR, XRD, and SEM analysis. BET obtained the surface area and the pore size was about 8.39 m2/g and 4.5 nm, respectively. Inspired by the published research and to expand the application fields of Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs ternary hybrid, it was selected as a platform for the co-delivery of CUR and DOX. The CUR and DOX content of this carrier was 54.2% and 43.2%, respectively. The drug release results demonstrate the higher stimuli-responsibility of Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs against pH variation, so, the nanocarriers released DOX@CUR in a controlled manner at the simulated acidic pH of cancer cells. There was no toxicity effect for Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs in the dose-dependent evaluation, so it was cytocompatible. MTT results showed that the dual drug-loaded prepared biocompatible Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs have a higher cytotoxic effect on the (cell viability of 23.5%) compared to free CUR@DOX (cell viability of 27.0%) in cancer cells killing after drug loading on it. As expected, DAPI as an advanced cellular test also confirmed this finding. It is believed that the results of current research like the use of low-cost, more available, and biocompatible reagents for the short synthesis of a new system with the desired porous structure for drug loading, pH-sensitive release behavior, and nontoxicity with accelerate the introduction of the new biomaterials based drug delivery systems for medical applications be propitious and open a good route for researchers of this interesting field. The results suggested that the reported Bio-MOF(Zn)@CMS/GQDs ternary hybrid is a safe nanovaluable choice in biomedicine and displayed great potential in drug delivery and tumor therapy.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research is supported by a research grant of the University of Tabriz (number SAD/3930-14001225).
